title: Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson - Judgment

A. Factual and procedural background
1. This ma(cid:425)er has a long and complex procedural history, which it is necessary that I set out in order
to explain the context for the hearings before me on 31 July, and 16 and 18 September 2025, and in
respect of which I now give this judgment. It is also likely to be useful that I do so for the benefit of
any judge in the future who may have to deal with further applica(cid:415)ons in this or any related ma(cid:425)ers.
2. In preparing this survey of the background history, I have drawn on the evidence before me and the
previous wri(cid:425)en judgments in this ma(cid:425)er of Mangatal J dated 21 September 2015 and of Williams J
dated 16 October 2019.
3. The substan(cid:415)ve proceedings are a probate and administra(cid:415)on ac(cid:415)on concerning the estate of Mr
Layman Hopkin Ebanks. Mr Ebanks died on 2 March 1992, leaving a widow and three children. Mr
Layman Ebanks’ children are the Plain(cid:415)ff and the Respondents in these proceedings. Mr Ebanks’
estate included a property at Registra(cid:415)on Sec(cid:415)on West Bay, Block 4D, Parcel 277 (“the Estate
Property”). Ini(cid:415)ally, le(cid:425)ers of administra(cid:415)on were granted in favour of Mr Ebanks’ widow, Mrs Vera
Ebanks. However, she died in October 1998 without having completed the administra(cid:415)on of the
estate. In 1999, Ms Mitchell-Johonson, the First Respondent, obtained a further grant of le(cid:425)ers of
administra(cid:415)on in her favour so that she could complete the administra(cid:415)on of Mr Ebanks’ estate.
4. By 2014, Mr Ronnie Ebanks, the Plain(cid:415)ff, had become dissa(cid:415)sfied with Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s
conduct as administratrix. By an origina(cid:415)ng summons dated 15 July 2014, Mr Ebanks applied to
remove Ms Mitchell-Johonson as administratrix and sought an order that he be appointed as
administrator in her place. At that (cid:415)me, Mr Ebanks was represented by Samson & McGrath and
Ms Mitchell-Johonson was represented by Dinner Mar(cid:415)n. On 15 October 2014, Williams J made
interlocutory orders for Ms Mitchell-Johonson to provide an account of her dealings with Mr Layman
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 2 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 3 of 29 2026-03-25
Ebanks’ estate and accepted her undertaking not to transfer the Estate Property nor to grant any
further charges over it. This la(cid:425)er requirement appears to have been included because Ms Mitchell-
Johonson had granted a charge over the Estate Property in favour of JN Cayman to secure repayment
of money she had borrowed in her personal capacity.
5. Mangatal J heard Mr Ebanks’ origina(cid:415)ng summons on 12, 13 and 15 May 2015, and reserved her
judgment. Mr Ebanks was an inmate at HMP Northward at the (cid:415)me, and the learned judge had
concerns whether he was an appropriate person to be appointed as administrator for that reason.
On 21 September 2015, the ma(cid:425)er was listed before Mangatal J for judgment. Mangatal J made two
Orders on 21 September 2015. The first was a Consent Order in the form of a dra(cid:332) that had been
filed by the par(cid:415)es on 27 July 2015. The Consent Order is at the heart of the ma(cid:425)ers currently before
the Court. So far as relevant, it provides as follows:
“AND UPON THE RESPONDENT UNDERTAKING to pay the debt owed to the Cayman
Islands Government for the medical bills of Layman Hopkin Ebanks totaling CI$94,682.95
IT IS HEREBY ORDERED BY CONSENT that:-
1. [Ms Mitchell-Johonson] is removed as Administratrix of the Estate of Layman Hopkin
Ebanks.
2. [Mr Ebanks] is appointed as Administrator of the Estate of Layman Hopkin Ebanks.
3. All existing charges against the Property are to be removed by [Ms Mitchell-Johonson]
within 90 days of this Order.
4. Title to the property Registration Section West Bay at Block 4D, Parcel 277 (“the
Property”) is to be transferred from [Ms Mitchell-Johonson] into the Estate of Layman
Hopkin Ebanks within 90 days of this Order or within 7 days of a Final Order, whichever
date is later.
5. A new charge in favour of [Ms Mitchell-Johonson] is to be made against the Property at
the time of transfer of title in such sum as the Court deems [Ms Mitchell-Johonson] to be
a creditor of the Estate.”
The reference to exis(cid:415)ng charges over the Estate Property is to the charge in favour of JN Cayman
granted by Ms Mitchell-Johonson.
6. The second Order made by Mangatal J on 21 September 2015 was not by consent and was
Mangantal J’s order on the aspects of Mr Ebanks’ origina(cid:415)ng summons that remained in conten(cid:415)on.
So far as material, it provides:
“AND UPON THE CONSENT ORDER filed on 27 July 2015
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 3 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 4 of 29 2026-03-25
AND UPON [MS MITCHELL-JOHONSON] UNDERTAKING to pay the debt owed to the
Cayman Islands Government for the medical bills of Layman Hopkin Ebanks totaling
CI$94,682.95
IT IS HEREBY ORDERED that:-
1. [Ms Mitchell-Johonson], as at the date of the interim account of 1 March 2015, is a
creditor of the Estate in the sum of CI$120,073.35
2. Costs of the action to [Mr Ebanks] from Ms Mitchell-Johonson to be taxed if not agreed
on the standard basis.”
7. Mangatal J also delivered a wri(cid:425)en judgment on Mr Ebanks’ origina(cid:415)ng summons on the same day.
Her judgment indicates that she had ordered in May 2015 that the Second Respondent should be
added to the proceedings. She records in her judgment that it was agreed by all par(cid:415)es that the
Second Respondent had been paid out her share of the estate as a result of the transfer to her in or
before 1998 of another property at Registra(cid:415)on Sec(cid:415)on West Bay at Block 4D, Parcel 276. The
Second Respondent therefore appears to have taken no part in the subsequent proceedings,
although it is unclear whether she was formally removed as a party.
8. I do not have evidence before me regarding Mr Ebanks’ conduct as administrator from 2015
onwards. However, he remained an inmate at HMP Northward for a large part of this period, so it is
possible that this interfered with his ability to deal with the estate.
9. Mr Ebanks says that on 6 February 2019, he discovered from correspondence with the Land Registry
that Ms Mitchell-Johonson had not complied with any of the requirements of the Consent Order,
and that the sum charged against the Estate Property in favour of JN Cayman had increased to
US $182,927. I will come back later to the reasons that Ms Mitchell-Johonson puts forward for these
admi(cid:425)ed failures on her part.
10. The judgment of Williams J dated 16 October 2019, which is the second key aspect to the ma(cid:425)ers
now before me, provides the following informa(cid:415)on regarding events in 2019. On 31 July 2019,
Mr Ebanks swore an affidavit in support of an applica(cid:415)on to commit Ms Mitchell-Johonson to prison
for contempt of court as a result of her failure to comply with the Consent Order. Mr Ebanks’ No(cid:415)ce
of Mo(cid:415)on was filed on 8 August 2019 and was listed for hearing on 15 October 2019. By that (cid:415)me,
Ms Mitchell-Johonson was living in the United States of America. However, she s(cid:415)ll had Cayman
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 4 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 5 of 29 2026-03-25
a(cid:425)orneys on the record as ac(cid:415)ng for her in rela(cid:415)on this ma(cid:425)er. On 29 August 2019, Mr Ebanks’
a(cid:425)orneys served the No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on and affidavit in support upon Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s Cayman
a(cid:425)orneys. Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s Cayman a(cid:425)orneys sent her a copy of the No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on by
email on 10 September 2019. However, Ms Mitchell-Johonson failed to engage with them regarding
their fees. On 2 October 2019, they warned her by email of the need to give her a(cid:425)en(cid:415)on to
Mr Ebanks’ applica(cid:415)on to commit her and advised her to obtain assistance from other a(cid:425)orneys.
Ms Mitchell-Johonson acknowledged that email.
11. On 10 October 2019, Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s a(cid:425)orneys successfully applied to come off the record
as ac(cid:415)ng for her in these proceedings. On 11 October 2019, Mr Ebanks’ a(cid:425)orneys re-served the
No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on and Mr Ebanks’ affidavit on Ms Mitchell-Johonson directly by email to the email
address that her former a(cid:425)orneys had used to reach her. On 14 October 2019, Ms Mitchell-Johonson
confirmed to Mr Ebanks’ a(cid:425)orneys that she had received the No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on and affidavit and
indicated that she was applying for legal aid.
12. Mr Ebanks’ No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on came before Williams J on 15 October 2019. Ms Mitchell-Johonson did
not a(cid:425)end, whether in person or by video link, and she was not represented at the hearing.
Williams J gave an extempore judgment, which was perfected on 16 October 2019.
13. Williams J summarised the relevant background, and made the following per(cid:415)nent observa(cid:415)on:
“4. The Final Order was filed on 21 September 2015, and therefore the date for compliance was
within 90 days of 21 September 2015, sometime in December 2015, almost 3¾ years ago. As it
was a consent order, Mangatal J who approved the Order and this Court are entitled to take the
view that the 1st Respondent, who was represented by Counsel at that hearing, was indicating to
the Court that she was willing and able to comply with the terms of the Order within the agreed
time frame. No evidence has been placed by the 1st Respondent before this Court to refute that.”
He con(cid:415)nued at paragraph 7 that:
“7. […] Correspondence was also received by the Petitioner’s attorneys from JN Cayman’s
attorneys on 30 July 2019 stating that it was hoped that a sale of Block 1D, Parcel 58 would
generate sufficient funds to redeem the mortgage which would leave the [Estate Property]
unencumbered. […] This evidence establishes that after 21 September 2015 the 1st Respondent
could and should have sold Parcel 58, which did not have negative equity, thereby releasing the
charge on the [Estate Property] as agreed in the Order. She would thereby have had the ability
to have the charge removed and to transfer the [Estate Property] if she had acted accordingly.”
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 5 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 6 of 29 2026-03-25
I will refer to Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s own property, which was also charged to JN Cayman, as “Parcel
58”.
14. Under the heading “Procedural Formali(cid:415)es” Williams J directed himself in paragraph 8 of his
judgment as to the requirement for strict compliance with procedural rules, by reference to the
English Court of Appeal’s judgment in Re L (A Child); In the Ma(cid:425)er of Gous Oddin [2016] EWCA Civ
173. Williams J then went through the efforts that had been made to bring the hearing and its
seriousness to Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s a(cid:425)en(cid:415)on. He set out the details of the communica(cid:415)ons with
Ms Mitchell-Johonson and her a(cid:425)orneys and was sa(cid:415)sfied that Ms Mitchell-Johonson was aware of
the hearing before him and of its serious nature, and that she was aware that she needed to address
the No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on urgently and to seek legal assistance. Williams J recorded that he had checked
with the Director of Legal Aid on 15 October 2019 and had been told that Ms Mitchell-Johonson did
not have an outstanding applica(cid:415)on for legal aid. He also noted that Ms Mitchell-Johonson had not
made any applica(cid:415)on to adjourn the hearing or to explain her non-a(cid:425)endance and had not provided
any substan(cid:415)ve response to the ma(cid:425)ers raised by Mr Ebanks’ No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on.
15. Williams J considered carefully whether he ought to adjourn the hearing. He held that Ms Mitchell-
Johonson had known of the hearing since 10 September 2019 and had failed to engage with it for
five weeks, and that he was sa(cid:415)sfied in the circumstances that it was appropriate to proceed to hear
the No(cid:415)ce of Mo(cid:415)on in Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s absence.
16. However, it is right to record that Williams J did not note or address in his considera(cid:415)on of the
procedural requirements the fact that the Consent Order did not include a penal no(cid:415)ce. It is unclear
from Williams J’s judgment whether this was raised with him during the hearing but it seems unlikely,
otherwise I feel it is likely that he would have commented upon it, if only to say why the need for a
penal no(cid:415)ce should be waived.
17. Williams J then considered the substance of Mr Ebanks’ complaints and concluded that he was sure
that Ms Mitchell-Johonson had failed to comply with paragraphs 3 and 4 of the Consent Order dated
21 September 2015 in that she had not removed all charges against the Estate Property within 90
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 6 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 7 of 29 2026-03-25
days or at all and had not transferred (cid:415)tle to the Estate Property to Mr Ebanks. In his conclusory
paragraphs, Williams J said this:
“20. […] I find that, over the past 3¼ years, there has been ample opportunity for [Ms Mitchell-
Johonson] to comply with the Order which she had agreed would and could be complied with
within 90 days of 21 September 2015. Having regard to the 30 July 2019 correspondence from
JN Cayman, it is evident that there was sufficient equity in the property at Block 1D, Parcel 58,
which if she had sold the same, would have enabled her to comply with the Order and release the
Charge and then transfer the Property. She has had the ability to comply with the Order after
September 2015.
”21. Having regard to her non-compliance with the terms of the Order over many years and
having regard to the information before me, I am satisfied that [Ms Mitchell-Johonson] has
wilfully failed to comply with the Order.”
18. Williams J therefore held that Ms Mitchell-Johonson should be commi(cid:425)ed to prison for contempt of
court for 28 days. However, he suspended the commi(cid:425)al for 28 days in order to give Ms Mitchell-
Johonson a final opportunity to comply with the Consent Order. He also gave Ms Mitchell-Johonson
liberty to apply to extend the suspension or to discharge the commi(cid:425)al order. This was on the basis
that it appeared that JN Cayman was seeking to exercise its power of sale of Parcel 58 due to
Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s default on her loan, which might allow the release of the charge over the
Estate Property.
19. Mr Ebanks’ a(cid:425)orneys filed the Order embodying Williams J’s decision on 16 October 2019. Williams J
also signed a warrant for Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s commi(cid:425)al dated 15 October 2019.
20. On 4 November 2019, Ms Mitchell-Johonson was granted legal aid limited to dealing with the
applica(cid:415)on to commit her for contempt. The grant of legal aid indicates that Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s
applica(cid:415)on was submi(cid:425)ed before 28 October 2019 but does not indicate precisely when it was
made. Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s evidence in her affidavit is that she submi(cid:425)ed her applica(cid:415)on to the
Legal Aid office on 14 October 2019. However, that is difficult to reconcile with the posi(cid:415)ve finding
by Williams J on 15 October 2019, following his enquiry with the Director of Legal Aid that morning,
that Ms Mitchell-Johonson had not made any applica(cid:415)on for legal aid by that date. To the extent
that it may be relevant, I conclude that Ms Mitchell-Johonson submi(cid:425)ed her applica(cid:415)on for legal aid
no earlier than 14 October 2019 and, if it was submi(cid:425)ed on 14 October 2019, it was not considered
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 7 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 8 of 29 2026-03-25
or determined by the legal aid office un(cid:415)l a(cid:332)er Williams J had completed the hearing on 15 October
2019.
21. Having obtained legal aid, Ms Mitchell-Johonson engaged Phillip Ebanks as her a(cid:425)orney at law. On
12 November 2019, Ms Mitchell-Johonson swore an affidavit in support of an applica(cid:415)on for a
further suspension of the warrant for commi(cid:425)al sta(cid:415)ng that she had been diagnosed with cancer in
March 2015 and had undergone a course of chemotherapy. She said that she had lost her job in
February 2018 due to her ill health. She said she had le(cid:332) the Cayman Islands on 28 October 2018 to
live in the USA and was applying for a green card and therefore could not leave the USA un(cid:415)l her
applica(cid:415)on had been decided. She said that, for these reasons, she had a reasonable excuse for not
complying with the Consent Order. Ms Mitchell-Johonson also provided some details regarding her
applica(cid:415)on for legal aid.
22. Mr Ebanks consented to Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s applica(cid:415)on for a further suspension of the warrant
for 28 days, which was embodied in an Order dated 15 November 2019. At the same (cid:415)me, Mr Ebanks
exchanged correspondence with the a(cid:425)orneys for JN Cayman, who indicated on 26 November 2019
that JN Cayman had now obtained an updated valua(cid:415)on of Parcel 58 and did not agree to release its
charge over the Estate Property because the value of Parcel 58 was insufficient to cover the full
amount that Ms Mitchell-Johonson owed.
23. It appears that Mr Ebanks did not pursue the ma(cid:425)er following receipt of this informa(cid:415)on un(cid:415)l 2024.
As Ms Mitchell-Johonson was not within the Cayman Islands and does not appear to have returned
to the Cayman Islands since October 2019, the warrant for her commi(cid:425)al appears never to have
been executed.
24. In 2023, Mr Ebanks’ a(cid:425)orneys successfully applied to come off the record. In April 2024, in light of
the lack of progress with the ma(cid:425)er since November 2019, the Court contacted Mr Ebanks to
enquire whether he intended to pursue it and fixed a hearing on 11 July 2024 of its own mo(cid:415)on for
a review. Mr Ebanks indicated that he did intend to proceed and was seeking to obtain legal aid and
legal representa(cid:415)on. The review hearing before me on 11 July 2024 was not effec(cid:415)ve as Mr Ebanks
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 8 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 9 of 29 2026-03-25
had not given no(cid:415)ce of the hearing to Ms Mitchell-Johonson (or to the Second Respondent) and the
court did not have any contact details for them. I suggested to Mr Ebanks that as a first step, he
might wish to file and serve a No(cid:415)ce of Inten(cid:415)on to Proceed given the (cid:415)me elapsed since the last
step in the proceedings.
25. On 27 August 2024, Mr Ebanks filed a No(cid:415)ce of Inten(cid:415)on to Proceed. The ma(cid:425)er was listed for
hearing on 31 October 2024, but this was again ineffec(cid:415)ve as Mr Ebanks had s(cid:415)ll not served
Ms Mitchell-Johonson with the no(cid:415)ce of hearing. On 4 November 2024, the Court no(cid:415)fied the
par(cid:415)es that the ma(cid:425)er would be listed for a further hearing on 5 December 2024. At the hearing on
that date, Ms Mitchell-Johonson appeared by video link from the USA and, at her request, I
adjourned the ma(cid:425)er for two months to allow her the opportunity to obtain legal advice.
26. The proceedings came back before me on 6 May 2025 for direc(cid:415)ons. Mr Ebanks appeared in person,
with Ms Kathleen Ryan ac(cid:415)ng as his McKenzie friend. Ms Mitchell-Johonson was represented by
Ms Ka(cid:427)na Anglin. I ordered that Mr Ebanks file an affidavit to support his applica(cid:415)on to enforce the
warrant for commi(cid:425)al. Ms Anglin indicated during the hearing that Ms Mitchell-Johonson wished to
make an applica(cid:415)on to set aside the warrant for commi(cid:425)al. I therefore ordered that Ms Mitchell-
Johonson should issue, file and serve any such applica(cid:415)on by 10 June 2025 as well as any suppor(cid:415)ng
evidence. On 27 May 2025, Ms Mitchell-Johonson filed her applica(cid:415)on to set aside the warrant for
commi(cid:425)al and suppor(cid:415)ng evidence.
27. The ma(cid:425)er was next before me on 31 July 2025, when it was listed for a final hearing. However, it
very quickly became apparent that Ms Anglin, who appeared on behalf of Ms Mitchell-Johonson,
wished to argue certain grounds of challenge to the warrant for commi(cid:425)al which were not raised in
Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s summons and which had not been no(cid:415)fied to Mr Ebanks or to Ms Ryan.
These related to alleged procedural defects in how the warrant was obtained from Williams J. I
considered it would be unfair to Mr Ebanks to allow Ms Anglin to raise these new ma(cid:425)ers at that
late stage. Ms Anglin therefore applied for an adjournment, which I granted to 16 September 2025
for Ms Mitchell-Johonson to amend her summons and ordered that Ms Mitchell-Johonson should
pay the costs thrown away by the adjournment.
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson
Page 9 of 29
P0004/1999 2026-03-25

P0004/1999 Page 10 of 29 2026-03-25
28. The substan(cid:415)ve hearing therefore look place on 16 and 18 September 2025, when I reserved
judgment. In the course of preparing my reserved judgment, I considered it important to provide
the par(cid:415)es with the opportunity to make further submissions on two English cases referred to within
a paragraph of the editorial notes in The Supreme Court Prac(cid:415)ce 1999, which was relied upon by Ms
Anglin. Ms Anglin provided some further wri(cid:425)en submissions on 10 February 2026 and Ms Ryan
indicated on 25 February 2026 that Mr Ebanks does not wish to make any further submissions in
response.
B. The applica(cid:415)ons by the par(cid:415)es
29. My ini(cid:415)al understanding from the hearings in 2024 and early 2025 was that Mr Ebanks wished to
enforce the warrant for Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s commi(cid:425)al that was signed by Williams J on 15
October 2019. However, his affidavit sworn on 20 May 2025 suggests that he wishes to apply instead
for a fresh warrant for Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s commi(cid:425)al. So far as material, his affidavit says:
“I, Ronnie, Rodney Ebanks, of HMP Northward Prison, make this affidavit in support of my
application to commit the First Respondent, Effie Mitchell-Johnson, to prison for a period of 28
days or until lawfully discharged, if sooner. This application is made pursuant to a Notice of
Motion dated 8 August 2019 and filed on 9 August 2019, seeking an order for the committal of
the 1st Respondent for contempt of court.
This application arises from the 1st Respondent's failure to comply with the Order of the Grand
Court dated 21 September 2015 (“the Order”). Under that Order, the Court directed—by
consent—that the 1st Respondent be removed as Administratrix of the Estate of the late Layman
Hopkin Ebanks. At paragraph 3 of the Order, the Court specifically directed the 1st Respondent
to remove all existing charges against the property registered as West Bay Block 4D Parcel 277,
within 90 days. As of today's date, she has failed to comply with that Order.
In my affidavit dated 31 July 2019, filed in support of the Notice of Motion, I asserted that the 1st
Respondent had willfully refused or neglected to comply with the Court's directive. I repeat that
assertion: the 1st Respondent has failed or refused to remove the charges as required.
[…]
In conclusion, I respectfully request that the Court:
1. Commit the 1st Respondent to prison for 28 days or until lawfully discharged for
contempt of court;
2. Order that all existing claims or liabilities against West Bay Block 4D Parcel 277, arising
from the 1st Respondent' s unlawful actions, be deemed personal liabilities of the 1st
Respondent and be removed from the Estate.”
I will return to Mr Ebanks’ applica(cid:415)on a(cid:332)er considering Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s summons.
[2026] CIGC (Civ) 14 – Ebanks v Mitchell-Johonson

P0004/1999 2026-03-25

30. Ms Anglin issued and filed Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s amended summons on 7 August 2025. The
grounds for relief that Ms Mitchell-Johonson specifies in her amended summons are as follows:
“1. The Warrant For Committal, issued by the Honourable Justice Richard Willams on 15th
October 2019, be set aside on the grounds that:-
a. The Warrant for Committal is defective due to non-compliance with:
i. GCR O45r7, which stipulates the prerequisites to enforcement of an order under
GCR O45r5, specifically that Justice Mangatal’s Order dated 21 September 2015
did not have the requisite penal notice required by GCR O45 r7(4)(a), that Order
being the Order the Petitioner sought to enforce by way of Warrant of Committal
under GCR O 52;
ii. The defect occasioned by the procedural irregularity set out at paragraph 1(a)(i)
above is of a nature and degree that the defect is not curable.
b. The Consent Order subsequently proved to be impossible for the Respondent to comply
with.
c. The administrator of the estate failed to take reasonable steps to discharge the
mortgage at the Respondent’s expense.”
31. I heard Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s applica(cid:415)on to set aside the warrant for commi(cid:425)al on 16 September
2025, when the par(cid:415)es did not complete their arguments, and reconvened on 18 September 2025
to complete the hearing.
C. Ms Mitchell-Johonson’s applica(cid:415)on to set aside the exis(cid:415)ng warrant for commi(cid:425)al
32. Ms Anglin, who appears on behalf of Ms Mitchell-Johonson, raises three reasons why she says the
warrant for commi(cid:425)al should be set aside. The first is that GCR O.45, r.7(4) requires that any order
